skill.name: extract
skill.description: Extract clean markdown or text from specific web URLs through Tavily's extraction API and MCP endpoint.
skill.summary: Extract readable web page content from known URLs using Tavily.
skill.author: tavily-ai

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly true positives for shell execution, Tavily network access, credential use, and hidden OAuth cache reads, but they align with the skill's stated extraction purpose. I found no evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration to unrelated domains, destructive filesystem behavior, or confirmed malicious intent. The skill should publish with warnings because it handles Tavily tokens, reads local MCP auth files, starts an OAuth helper with npx, and sends requested URLs to Tavily.

medium_findings.title: Tavily Token Access and Forwarding
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The script reads TAVILY_API_KEY or a Tavily OAuth token from the local MCP auth cache, then uses it as a bearer token for the Tavily MCP endpoint. This is expected for the skill, but it means the skill handles sensitive credentials and should be used only with trusted Tavily endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads a local OAuth token or environment API key and sends it in an Authorization header. The destination is the documented Tavily MCP endpoint, so the risk is real but contextually expected.

medium_findings.title: Runtime External Command Use
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The shell script invokes jq, find, base64, date, curl, and npx. Most arguments are controlled or quoted, but runtime command execution increases supply chain and environment risk, especially when npx starts mcp-remote during OAuth.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the shell script and support the stated workflow. I did not find unquoted user input used as a shell command, so command injection confidence is low while command execution risk is medium.

medium_findings.title: User URLs Sent to External Service
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill accepts user-provided URLs and sends them to Tavily for extraction. This is the core capability, but users should understand that target URLs and extraction queries are shared with Tavily.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request body is built from the user's JSON input and posted to Tavily. This is expected behavior, but it has clear privacy implications for submitted URLs and queries.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Static Network and Command Findings
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many SKILL.md findings are examples inside documentation, including curl commands and Tavily URLs. They describe how to call the API and are not executed by the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside markdown code fences and reference tables. It is instructional content, not executable code loaded by the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Heuristic Is Expected Token Validation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static system reconnaissance alerts map to normal shell checks, token expiry comparison, and local auth cache discovery. I found no evidence of broad host inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code checks JWT issuer, JWT expiry, and JSON validity. These operations support authentication and input validation rather than host reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Heuristic Has No Supporting Code
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged SKILL.md lines do not implement a weak cryptographic algorithm. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, custom encryption, or cryptographic decision logic in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are a natural language description and an API request field table. They contain no cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden OAuth Cache Read
dangerous_patterns.description: The script searches HOME/.mcp-auth for token files. This is legitimate for OAuth reuse, but hidden credential stores should be read only for the intended Tavily issuer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden directory read is explicit, and the script validates the issuer before reuse. The pattern is sensitive even though the implementation includes a meaningful guard.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Call with Bearer Credential
dangerous_patterns.description: The script sends an Authorization bearer value to https://mcp.tavily.com/mcp. This should be acceptable only because Tavily is the expected service for this skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and Tavily endpoint are directly present in the script. I found no alternate external endpoint receiving the credential.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime npx OAuth Helper
dangerous_patterns.description: The script starts mcp-remote through npx when no Tavily token is present. This creates runtime dependency and package resolution risk during authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The npx invocation is explicit and used for OAuth. It is not evidence of malicious behavior, but runtime package execution is a valid marketplace warning.

content.user_title: Extract Web Page Content with Tavily
content.value_statement: Web pages are hard to reuse when you only need clean article or documentation text. This skill sends selected URLs to Tavily and returns extracted markdown or plain text for analysis.
actual_capabilities: Extracts readable content from one or more supplied URLs.
Supports markdown or plain text output through Tavily options.
Can focus extraction with a query and chunks per source.
Supports basic and advanced extraction depth options.
Can include image URLs when the request enables image extraction.
Uses Tavily API key or Tavily OAuth token authentication.
limitations: Requires a Tavily account and valid authentication.
Sends submitted URLs and extraction options to Tavily.
Cannot create a Tavily account through the OAuth flow.
Extraction quality depends on page access, page structure, and Tavily service behavior.

use_cases.title: Collect Source Material
use_cases.description: Extract readable text from known articles before summarizing or comparing them.

use_cases.title: Read Vendor Documentation
use_cases.description: Pull focused sections from documentation pages for troubleshooting or implementation planning.

use_cases.title: Prepare Competitive Notes
use_cases.description: Extract clean content from public product pages for review and internal analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Extract One Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single known URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the main content from this URL with Tavily and return concise markdown: https://example.com/article

prompt_templates.title: Extract Multiple Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Tavily extract on these URLs and summarize the key points from each page: https://example.com/page1 and https://example.com/page2

prompt_templates.title: Focus on a Topic
prompt_templates.scenario: Target only relevant sections from long pages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract these URLs with a query focus on authentication setup. Return the most relevant chunks and cite the source URL for each point.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Complex Pages
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced extraction for dynamic or structured pages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use advanced Tavily extraction for this JavaScript-heavy page, include image URLs if available, and report any failed extraction results.

output_examples.input: Extract a public documentation page about authentication.
output_examples.output: The result contains the page title, clean markdown sections, setup steps, and any failed extraction notice.

output_examples.input: Extract three articles with a query about model evaluation.
output: Source one: relevant extracted passages about evaluation criteria.
Source two: relevant extracted passages about benchmark design.
Source three: relevant extracted passages about deployment monitoring.

output_examples.input: Extract a dynamic product page using advanced depth.
output_examples.output: The result includes readable page content, visible structured sections, and optional image links when Tavily returns them.

best_practices: Use specific URLs that you are allowed to process.
Use a query when only part of a long page is relevant.
Try basic extraction first, then use advanced extraction for dynamic pages.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private URLs or sensitive query text unless Tavily processing is approved.
Do not use this skill as a crawler for large unbounded site scans.
Do not assume extraction results are complete without checking failed results.

faq.question: Does this skill need a Tavily account?
faq.answer: Yes. It needs a Tavily account and either OAuth access or a Tavily API key.

faq.question: Where does the extracted URL data go?
faq.answer: The script sends the submitted URLs and options to Tavily for extraction.

faq.question: Can it extract more than one page?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented request supports multiple URLs, with a stated maximum of 20 per request.

faq.question: Can it focus on a topic within a page?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the query option and chunks per source to request relevant content.

faq.question: Does it support JavaScript-heavy pages?
faq.answer: Yes. The advanced extraction depth is intended for dynamic or complex pages.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with warnings. It handles credentials, reads an OAuth cache, runs shell commands, and contacts Tavily.
